sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 62-year-old male, presents with a nonhealing foot ulcer on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, which has been present for approximately two months.
The ulcer has recently become red and is draining, causing the patient concern about its healing.
The patient reports walking barefoot on the beach as a possible cause of the injury.
He denies any trauma to the foot.
The patient has not picked up antibiotics prescribed by his primary care physician (PCP) yet and does not recall the name of the medication.
He reports occasional throbbing pain and redness around the ulcer.
The patient has a history of diabetes, which he believes is not well-controlled, with a reported A1c of around 10%.
He takes medication for diabetes but does not regularly check his blood sugar levels.
Past medical history: Diabetes.
Review of systems: The patient reports occasional throbbing pain in the foot.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Normal temperature, no fever.
Physical exam: A one by two inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth MTP joint, with redness, drainage, edema, and fluid present.
No necrosis, odor, or bony exposure is appreciated.
The patient reports increased pressure when the wound is touched, but no distinct pain.
Test Results: No evidence of osteomyelitis on an x-ray of the right foot.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with signs of infection, including redness, drainage, and edema.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Infection; Diabetes mellitus (active).
Plan:
I ordered an ABI (ankle-brachial index) to determine the blood supply to the foot and assess the ability to heal the ulcer.
I performed a debridement to remove dead tissue from the wound.
I prescribed clindamycin 400mg twice daily for 7 days to treat the infection.
I also arranged for a surgical shoe to be provided to the patient to reduce pressure on the foot and promote healing.
The patient will return in two weeks for another debridement and assessment of the wound's progress.
Follow-up: The patient will return in two weeks for follow-up and possible debridement.
The patient is advised to contact the office if any issues arise before the scheduled appointment.
